coredata.dc:title: A photothermal and pH-responsive intelligent PSBG nanofiller for enhancing the barrier and self-healing performance of the SMP coatings
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xianliang
Gong, Bin
Zhang, Jie
Di, Yuyang
Ding, Yan
Chen, Zhihao
Yang, Wenzhong
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a protective smart coating with excellent light-thermal and pH dual-responsive can furnish excellent corrosion protection for aluminum alloys is prepared. This kind of composite coating incorporates a shape memory polymer (SMP) embedded with core-shell PDA/SiO2/BTA/GQDs (PSBG) nanofillers. The intelligent PSBG fillers agent are prepared by layer-by-layer assembly method, polydopamine-core (PDA-core) can achieve superior photothermal conversion ability, hollow SiO2 on PDA can carry a lot of BTA corrosion inhibitors, while graphene quantum dots (GQDs) cap acting as gatekeepers can regulate benzotriazole (BTA) leakage from hollow PDA/SiO2 nanocontainers. In this instance, on the one hand, PSBG nanofillers can sense and heal micro corrosion region, on the other hand, the outstanding light-thermal conversion effect of PSBG can enhance the coating temperature to awaken the barrier recovery effect as well as can trigger the release of carried inhibitors to quickly repress the corrosive redox reaction. To assess the self-healing capabilities of the composite coatings, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and surface morphology studies are used. According to the aforementioned findings, PSBG-based coatings possess a greater levels of corrosion resistance and an outstanding active protection capabilities than blank SMP. All in all, our research of pH-responsive and light-thermal double-triggered intelligent coating systems is successfully synthesized, and the findings supported the use of coatings based on nanofillers in the hopes of improving the life of corrosion protection.

head.ce:title: A photothermal and pH-responsive intelligent PSBG nanofiller for enhancing the barrier and self-healing performance of the SMP coatings

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Nanjing Tech University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a protective smart coating with excellent light-thermal and pH dual-responsive can furnish excellent corrosion protection for aluminum alloys is prepared. This kind of composite coating incorporates a shape memory polymer (SMP) embedded with core-shell PDA/SiO2/BTA/GQDs (PSBG) nanofillers. The intelligent PSBG fillers agent are prepared by layer-by-layer assembly method, polydopamine-core (PDA-core) can achieve superior photothermal conversion ability, hollow SiO2 on PDA can carry a lot of BTA corrosion inhibitors, while graphene quantum dots (GQDs) cap acting as gatekeepers can regulate benzotriazole (BTA) leakage from hollow PDA/SiO2 nanocontainers. In this instance, on the one hand, PSBG nanofillers can sense and heal micro corrosion region, on the other hand, the outstanding light-thermal conversion effect of PSBG can enhance the coating temperature to awaken the barrier recovery effect as well as can trigger the release of carried inhibitors to quickly repress the corrosive redox reaction. To assess the self-healing capabilities of the composite coatings, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and surface morphology studies are used. According to the aforementioned findings, PSBG-based coatings possess a greater levels of corrosion resistance and an outstanding active protection capabilities than blank SMP. All in all, our research of pH-responsive and light-thermal double-triggered intelligent coating systems is successfully synthesized, and the findings supported the use of coatings based on nanofillers in the hopes of improving the life of corrosion protection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: To improve the anti-corrosion capabilities of metallic materials, many protective coatings have been widely used [1]. While this is going on, industrial engineering is generally moving toward intellectualization, and corrosion prevention for metals may do the same. The concept of intelligent self-healing coatings that can organically repair surface damage and regain anti-corrosion qualities with little to no artificial help is similar to giving life to the coating. There is no denying that in recent years, intelligent self-repair coatings have caught the attention of numerous researchers [2].

Generally speaking, a promising self-healing strategy comprises an environmental spontaneously stimulation or only requires a little amount of manual intervention to start the coating's restoration [3,4]. For the environmental spontaneously self-healing strategy, the damaged coatings are autonomously repaired relying on the controllable release of corrosion inhibitors triggered by the metal’s corrosion reaction. To achieve the goal of self-healing, nanofillers with corrosion inhibitors are carried in the coating matrix that can be stimulated by the local corrosion environment (pH change or redox reaction). After that, the released corrosion inhibitors can rapidly uncover the corrosion site [5]. For example, Khan et al. prepared a pH-sensitive Elosh nanotubes, which successfully loaded two corrosion inhibitors by layer-by-layer packaging method. New hybrid material can sense changes in pH on the surface of the metal and release synergistic corrosion inhibitors, which can greatly reduce corrosion activity in its early stage of corrosion [6]. Silica nanofillers are chosen from a wide range of nanofillers as the best option for encapsulating micro-molecular active species for corrosion protection [7]. Numerous SiO2-based intelligent release materials have been demonstrated in recent literature, using layer-by-layer assembly technology, biomolecules, and acid-damaged structure as "responsive valves" that can react to environmental stimuli like high temperature, redox activation, and raise or lower pH changes [8].

Additionally, the shape memory polymers (SMP) coating can successfully inherit the advantages of the conventional solvent-borne or water-borne, etc. epoxy coatings, such as affordable, high adhesion, acceptable corrosion resistance, and extended chemical resistance [9,10]. Another important anti-corrosion repair strategy involves the SMP can be recovered utilizing reversible chemical bonds, polymeric chain rearrangements, or low melting point materials triggered by heat and light manual intervention [11,12]. Light-stimulated repair coating has many advantages such as it can be managed remotely, and the adjustable light (such as area and energy of the light) can trigger local self-healing performance. In this context, the development of a novel photothermal conversion-based stimulation technique in self-healing coatings is urgently required. More importantly, PDA as natural melanin polymers, show high adhesion for almost all nanomaterials because of its catechol structure [13]. PDA is seen as a potential ecologically acceptable agent for NIR light-triggered thermal repair coating in the meantime. Because of this, PDA can show significant photothermal conversion when exposed to NIR light [14,15]. These powerful photothermal materials can supply a significant amount of energy to the polymer chain, and within a short period of time, the coating's self-repairing program is completed by the non-covalent or covalent healing mechanism [11,16].

However, numerous studies have shown that a single spontaneously repair strategy, can repair defects spontaneously, but the finite nanofillers in the smart coating can only provide a low-concentration healing agent and can’t repair the same site many times [12]. For a single artificial intervention repair method, coatings can undergo multiple repairs, but strictly speaking, it lacks spontaneity healing functions thus long-term protection of metals is nearly impossible [15,17,18]. As a result, highly effective smart coatings with advanced collaborative trigger strategies are required.

In recent years, graphene (GO) has been proven that can strengthen the barrier qualities and mechanical stability of epoxy coatings. Additionally, the huge specific surface area can serve as a vessel to absorb significant amounts of corrosion inhibitors, accordingly, graphene is frequently employed as a filler inserted in organic coating systems [19,20]. For instance, Javidparvar et al. fabricated a graphene oxide loaded with two eco-friendly synergistic effect corrosion inhibitors (cerium ions and benzimidazole). According to this study, the barrier qualities and self-repair effectiveness of epoxy-polyamide coating can be greatly improved by the on-demand release of two corrosion inhibitors [21]. Li et al. propose an interesting approach to prepare a GO/polypyrrole-MOF superhydrophobic dual-layer coating, the presence of GO layer can offer a powerful barrier effect for improving the anti-corrosion capacity of Mg alloys [22]. Meanwhile, Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) being one of several important derivatives of graphene are rarely introduced in corrosion protection or coating substrate. Even though one of the main reasons GQDs cannot be used on a wide scale is their poor yield and low quantum yield, integrating the creation of GQDs through electrochemical synthesis, microwave irradiation, and solvothermal synthesis can successfully address this pressing issue [23]. Furthermore, in our view, GQDs have the following potential advantages in smart coating filed. GQDs typically have a smaller lateral dimension and consist of a few layers of flaky graphene [23]. They are high water dispersibility, high chemical stability, and low toxicity. More importantly, the carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl groups are typically abundant on the surface of GQDs [24–26]. This makes them easy to be loaded onto the surface of nanofillers, and it is the presence of these surface groups that can significantly increase the dispersibility and compatibility of nanofillers in the organic-coating, which will promote the physical barrier properties or anti-corrosion protection of the composites coating [27–29].

In this study, we integrated two self-healing coating technologies with a focus on achieving active anti-corrosion capacity and pays attention to coating's excellent recovered passive barrier effect. A core-shell structured PDA/SiO2/BTA/GQDs (PSBG) nanocontainers are facilely synthesized, among them PDA core (P) as a photothermal converter, due to the photothermal properties, it can generate a large amount of heat after absorbing NIR. The mesopores SiO2 on the PDA core nanocontainer can provide BTA molecules with better accommodation. GQDs valves are inlaid on the outside of PDA/SiO2 (PS) nanocontainers through a newly formed amide bond, which can ensure the effective encapsulation and controllable release of BTA inhibitors. Coincidentally, the amide bond between PS and GQDs can be destroyed by heat/NIR and pH= 3 or pH= 11 conditions [30,31], which will promote the release of corrosion inhibitors when metal is corrosion. To make a long story short, we have successfully endowed the SMP coatings to achieve dual-responsive self-healing reactions. This laboriously designed composite coating material is highly intelligent, extremely convenient, and can provide long-term protection, thus suiting metal corrosion protection in severe environments.

Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), benzotriazole (BTA), dopamine (DA), bisphenol a diglycidyl ether (DGEBA), neopentyl glycol diglycidyl ether (NGDE) and Jeffamine D230 curing agent are purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. Carnauba wax microparticles (SEM image is given in Fig. S1) are supplied by Beijing Huaweiruike Chemical Co., Ltd. Other reagents and solvents are supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent. All chemicals and solvents are of analytical grade and used as received. The solutions are prepared with ultrapure water of 18.2 MΩ cm.
The AA2024 corrosion test specimen (AA2024, 20 × 20 × 2 mm) is purchased from Shanghai Bixuan Metal Materials Co. Ltd., China. The corresponding element content is listed in Table 1.

The PDA/SiO2/BTA/GQDs are prepared through the layer-by-layer assembled method. Firstly, 1.5 ml of NH3·H2O (28 %) solution is added into a 130 ml mixed solution of ethanol: water = 4:9 (V1: V2), under mild stirring at 25 °C for 30 min. After that, 10 ml 50 mg/ml dopamine monomer solution is injected into the above mixture solution. The PDA core is obtained after 24 h of reaction, washed, and dried at 70 °C for 12 h in a vacuum oven.

After that, using a surfactant template approach, the SiO2 layer is deposited to the PDA core surface. Typically, 140 ml of water and ethanol mixed solution (water: ethanol =V1: V2 = 4:3) is used to disperse 300 mg of the PDA-core, 900 mg of CTAB, and 2 ml of NH3·H2O (28 %). 1.2 ml of the APTES and TEOS mixed solution (V1: V2 =1:3) are added after 30 min under ultrasonic. The PDA-SiO2 composites (PS) are collected and washed to remove any residual chemicals after 6 h of continuous stirring. The CTAB templates are then eliminated after refluxing in a 100 ml solution of methanol for 12 h at 75 °C. The synthesized PS nanocontainers are then dried at 60 °C in a vacuum oven.

After that, 40 ml of 50 mg/ml BTA aqueous solution is used to disperse 300 mg PS nanocontainers, and the vacuum method is used to carry out the encapsulation process. The above-mixed solution is maintained under a vacuum for 12 h to maximize the BTA inhibitors' ability to be encapsulated [32]. Finally, the PS that has been loaded with inhibitors is centrifuged and isolated, which is designated as PSB nanofillers.

Finally, the GQDs are further deposited as a cap to prevent the self-leakage of the encapsulated BTA inhibitors. Specifically, the GQDs are synthesized based on a bottom-up method [33]. Then, PSB is transferred into 10 ml ultrapure water and further added with 100 mg GQDs, 80 mg of 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), and 80 mg of N-Hydroxysuxxinimide (NHS) blend solution. The final PDA/SiO2/BTA/GQDs (PSBG) nanofillers are obtained following numerous washing and drying steps using the freeze-drying method, which takes place after 12 h of stirring in the dark.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of smart coating with different proportion nanofillers
ce:section.ce:para: To completely remove metallic oxides or impurities on the alloy surface, the AA2024 sheet is polished with abrasive papers and then washed by ethanol three times. As for the preparation of coating, 1 wt%, and 2 wt% amounts of PSBG are added into the epoxy matrix consisting of 2 g DGEBA and 2 g NGDE. After 5 min of stirring, 2 g D230 is added to the coating solution. Then, 1 g of carnauba wax microparticles as coating substrate is further mixed into the above coating colloid and keep stirring for 30 min. Finally, utilizing a stick applicator, the coatings are applied on the AA2024 working electrode and precured at 25 °C for 12 h before being absolutely cured at 55 °C for 24 h. Similarly, control group coatings are also prepared according to similar procedures. The thickness of all the dry coatings is controlled at approximately 130 ± 5 µm. All the coating is damaged by a scalpel that introduced scratches with a length of 0.5 cm, and a width is about 40 µm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and chemical structure characterization of the nanofillers and coating
ce:section.ce:para: Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Quanta FEG250, FEI) with a 15 kV accelerating voltage is used to observe the surface morphologies of the nanofillers and coating. The synthesized nanofillers' Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) are measured using a Nicolet iS5 spectrophotometer with KBr pellets. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Axis Supra, England) is used to determine the chemical composition. The Micromeritics ASAP 2460 analyzer in an N2 atmosphere confirms the nanofillers' surface area and pore volume. Malvern Zeta sizer Nano ZS90 at 25 °C investigates the Zeta potentials of synthetic nanofillers. Neutral aqueous dispersions with a concentration of 5 mg/ml constitute all of the tested nanofillers. The coating adhesion performance is measured according to the ASTM standard D3359–17.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive release test of the BTA corrosion inhibitors under different pH and temperature
ce:section.ce:para: Moreover, using UV–vis absorption spectra to study the responsive release mechanism of BTA from PSBG nanofillers by Lambda 1050+ (PerkinElmer) spectrophotometer. For details, 60 mg PSBG nanofillers and 15 ml 3.5 wt% NaCl solution (pH = 3, 7, 11) mixed solution is placed in a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut off = 3500). Finally, transfer the dialysis bag to a beaker containing 185 ml 3.5 wt% NaCl solution at the same pH values of 3, 7, and, 11 (adjusted by adding diluted HCl or NaOH solutions), meanwhile the solution outside the dialysis bag is continuously mixed at the speed of 300 revolutions by a magnetic stirrer. At different time intervals, a quartz cell containing 3 milliliters of test-extracted solutions is moved to measure the absorbance at 259 nm (Characteristic peak of BTA). To maintain the same solution concentration, 3 ml same pH value fresh solution is injected straightaway to the above beaker. All temperature changes are measured by thermocouple thermometers.

Firstly, the three-electrode system includes the 4 cm2 exposed area of AA2024 samples used as the working electrode, the counter electrode is a type 213 flakiness platinum electrode, and the reference electrode is a type 218 Ag/AgCl electrode. Gamry reference 3000 working station uses a steady open circuit potential (OCP) to test all EIS. The tests are conducted with a scanning frequency of 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz and a voltage amplitude of 10 mV. All scratch and healed coating samples are placed in a beaker with 250 ml neutral, 3.5 wt% NaCl and are observed for 1–21 days to determine the effectiveness of self-healing. Using Z Simp Win software, EIS data with chi-squared values less than 10−3 orders are fitted after tests. Moreover, salt spray test according to the ISO 7253 standard is further applied to test anti-corrosive performance of these composite coatings under harsh temperature, humidity and salinity conditions (the results and discussions are displayed in supporting).

Fig. 1 carefully demonstrates the preparation process of PSBG nanofillers, and Fig. 2 is the micromorphology of the synthesized nanofillers. The PDA-core (Fig. 2A) exhibits a spherical structure with an approximately 520 nm uniform diameter. The PDA-core contains a phenolic hydroxyl group, which gives it an adhesive effect on a variety of materials. As a result, the silica structure will self-adsorb on the PDA surface for further growth. As shown in Fig. 2B, the surface roughness increases evidently as the PDA surface is covered by the erratic SiO2 layer, in the meantime, the color of nanofillers changes from black to brown, further demonstrating the SiO2 layer's successful loading onto the PDA-core nanocontainers. In contrast to PDA-core, the PS nanocontainers appear to have a floral structure shape and have particle diameters of 620 nm or 640 nm. As a result, the thickness of the hollow silica layer is estimated to be roughly 50 nm. The findings suggest that PS nanocontainers can offer a significant amount of load area to hold BTA corrosion inhibitors. In Fig. 2C, the irregular flower-like structure of PS is retained after loading BTA and GQDS, and the morphology of PSBG nanofillers has essentially not changed in comparison to PS nanocontainers [34].

FTIR and XPS spectra provide additional confirmation of the chemical composition of nanomaterials. The characteristic peaks in the FTIR spectra ( Fig. 3) correspond to the stretching vibrations of the N-H, O-H, C-C, and C-N bands, respectively, at 1640 cm−1, 1342 cm−1, 1404 cm−1, and 1111 cm−1 [35], respectively. These distinctive clusters demonstrate that the PDA-core is successfully formed. Around 1064 cm−1, which corresponds to the stretching vibration of the Si-O bond, the peak's intensity for the PS sample considerably increased [36]. Moreover, the intensity of the major absorption bonds of the PDA-core decrease demonstrating the shielding effect of the SiO2 layer towards the bottom PDA-core [37]. The aforementioned findings demonstrate that the SiO2 shell that is currently covering the PDA-surface cores is properly deposited. In addition, there is an additional bond at 740 cm–1 in the PSBG patterns, which is attributed to the C–H bending vibration in the aromatic ring of the BTA inhibitor [38]. Meanwhile, the strong bond at 1689 cm–1 resulted from the combination of the carboxylic acid C＝O stretches in the GQDs [33]. The outcomes show that the capping off of the GQDs on the BTA-loaded PS nanofiller is successful. A further discovery we made is the abundance of carboxyl and amino groups in GQDs, which may help to enhance the compatibility of nanofillers with SMP polymers [39].

Fig. 4A exhibits the XPS spectra of P, PS, GQDs, and PSBG different signal intensities of C, N, O, and Si without other impurities. Especially, Fig. 4a compares the computed atomic ratios of the samples of P, PS, PSBG, and GQDs. Because of silane functionalization, the Si element can only be found in PS in comparison to P nanofillers. Moreover, the intensity of O 1s and Si 2p peaks both increase for PS. This confirms that SiO2 has been grafted on PDA-core nanocontainers. Here, we observe that a greater N 1s peak is seen for the PSBG, which is a result of the BTA and GQDs load. On the other hand, the O 1s peak has become stronger, showing that the GQDs cap has been successfully encapsulated on the surface of the nanofillers, which further proves that the PSBG preparation is successful.

Fig. 4 B, b, C, and c exhibit the high-resolution N 1s and C 1s spectrum of PS and PSBG nanofillers. For PS, the N 1s can be deconvoluted into two single peaks, –NH2 peak is mainly attributed to PDA and highly aminated SiO2 shell, and the significantly tall –N = peak pertains to the PDA structure [40]. The -NH2 peak is primarily related to PDA and the heavily aminated SiO2 shell. Deconvolution of the C 1s signal also revealed that C-C or C-N primarily came from the PDA layer in PS. The peak of N 1s widens and rises in the samples with PSBG nanofillers. A further fit is made to the new high peak at 399.31 eV, which is attributed to the nitrogen double bond in the triazole rings of BTA. On the other hand, the carboxyl group on the surface of GQDs is where the new peak CO in PSBG originates. The aforementioned occurrences demonstrate that GQD caps and BTA inhibitors are successfully deposited into PS nanocontainers.

After the deposition of each layer, the surface charge state is properly validated by a zeta potential test. The findings are shown in Fig. 5. Due to the deprotonation of the phenolic hydroxyl groups in PDA at neutral pH, the surface charge of pure PDA-core is 14.4 mV. Due to the presence of many aminoated silica structures on the surface of the PS layer after modification with an aminated SiO2 layer, the surface charge increased to positive 12.6 mV. Additionally, the grafting of BTA caused the surface charge of PSB to drop to a positive 7.5 mV. Because of the coordination reaction between the carboxyl groups on the surface of the GQDs caps and the amino groups of the PSB nanofillers, the surface charge of PSB after the reaction with GQDs increased obviously to a positive value of 30.6 mV. The PSBG's effective acquisition may also be indicated by the surface charge's considerable alterations. In the meanwhile, we can establish that electrostatic adsorption has attached the GQDs film to the PS nanocontainers that have been filled with BTA. Additionally, it is well known that a stronger electrostatic repulsion, which is more stable in the SMP colloids, is revealed by a higher absolute value of the zeta potential value of PSBG nanofiller.

The N2 adsorption-desorption curve of PS and PSBG samples is displayed in Fig. 6A to analyze the pore structure of nanofillers. Two nanofiller materials can be categorized as mesoporous materials based on the curve's shape [41]. Fig. 6A and Table 2 show that the specific surface area of PS nanocontainers is up to 659.25 m2⋅g−1 and the pore volume keeps a relatively high value of 0.41 cm3⋅g−1. The channel distribution curve (Fig. 6B) shows that the PS nanofillers’ mesoporous diameter is uniform and lengthen out to 3.93 nm. For large-capacity nanocontainers utilized for BTA storage and delivery, it is important to note that larger pore volume, better specific surface area, and wider mesoporous diameter are extremely promising. In comparison, after modification with GQDs caps, the PSBG nanofillers own the markedly declining value of the surface area, as well as the pores volume, decreased to 94.85 m2⋅g−1 and 0.26 cm3⋅g−1, respectively, providing that GQDs are successfully sealed the hole on the surface of the functionalized PS nanocontainers. In addition, the 3.93 nm pore size distribution of PS disappears in the mesopore range, indicating that the cap effect of GQDs may be able to reduce the desultory leaking of BTA corrosion inhibitors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heat and pH effect and inhibitor release from PSBG
ce:section.ce:para: The loading capacity is calculated according to the total amount of the BTA inhibitor as a percentage of the effective inhibitor loading on PSBG [42]. Afterward, based on the outcomes of the UV–vis test, it may be determined that the total weight of released BTA is 14.3 mg. Additionally, the weight of all PSBG nanofillers is 60 mg, hence it is estimated that 23.8 wt percent of PSBG can be loaded with BTA. The fact that the loading capability is a little bit higher than in the other studies [19,39,43], which could be attributable to the π – π stacking or electrostatic adsorption between PDA and BTA. As a result, the PS nanocontainer can hold more BTA molecules.

Fig. 7A displays the inhibitor-release properties. And the analogous releasing mechanism under various circumstances is shown in Fig. 7B. All in all, the BTA in PSBG exhibits pH and heat-dependent inhibitor release behavior. Within the 24 h period, the release of BTA from the PSBG exhibits a slower release rate and a low release concentration in the medium with a pH of 7.0. About 16 % of BTA are released at 25 °C. The low release rate is attributed to two main factors: on the one hand, GQDs can seal up the hollow PS nanofillers to limit BTA arbitrary leaking. On the other hand, BTA exhibit a low solubility in neutral solution compare with in acidic and alkaline solution. Nevertheless, fast-release behavior is discovered when the pH decreased to 3.0 and rose to 11.0 compared to pH= 7.0, and the released BTA achieved 98.5 % (pH=3) and 25.9 % (pH=11) at 25 °C, respectively. This obvious stimulus-response behavior might be attributed to the fact that when the pH is 7, the strong electrostatic attraction from –NH3 + of aminated PS nanocontainers and the –COO- groups of GQDs caps promoted GQDs getting close to PS nanocontainers. When pH is dramatically altered, the interaction between PS and GQDs caps changes from electrostatic attraction to electrostatic repulsion as a result of the protonation and deprotonation of -NH3 + and -COO- groups [31]. After that, PS nanocontainers take off the cap and BTA can easily dissolve in acidic and alkaline solution, resulting in more BTA being released from PSBG complexes. More importantly, in the same neutral pH environment, a high temperature (50 °C) slightly promotes the release rate of BTA than at a low temperature (25 °C), illustrating the interaction forces between GQDs and PS can be weakened by the high temperature. Also, high temperatures will also increase the transmission speed of BTA in mesoporous channels. Hence, the massive falling of the GQDs cap and high transmission speed caused a large amount of leakage of BTA in high-temperature environments [44,45].

In conclusion, the release of BTA from PSBG demonstrates pH and temperature-responsive properties, which can significantly increase the delivery efficiency of the corrosion inhibitors. In actuality, the goal of intelligent materials in the coating business has always been to provide such rich response forms of nanofillers. In this instance, local pH variations or photothermal stimulation can help the coating work better as a barrier when the alloy started to corrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal performance of PSBG nanofillers and PSBG coatings
ce:section.ce:para: As an important carbon-based polymer, PDA owns numerous positive characteristics, such as excellent environment-friendly, high hydrophilicity, and high biocompatibility, what is more important, PDA can effectively convert the NIR light to heat with light-thermal conversion efficiency (over 40 %) [46]. Fig. 8A shows the temperature rise curve of different nanoparticles in the aqueous solution. We observe that after 5 min, the temperature of pure H2O only increased by 5 °C under the NIR irradiation. For comparison, as the PDA sample curve shows, due to the PDA fully absorbing the light, PDA generates a large amount of heat per unit of time, in the 5 min irradiation the solution temperature shows a lot of improvement, elevated rapidly from 30 to 58 °C, compared with pure water. Due to the penetration of NIR light and the photothermy effect of mesoporous silica layers, the curve of the PS sample and that of the P sample maintained a similar heat production efficiency. Because GQDs can also convert NIR light into heat energy [47], the PSBG sample features a variety of potent photothermal conversion components. Consequently, this comparison clearly demonstrates that under the same conditions, the rising temperature of PSBG is higher than that of other samples. The aforementioned findings demonstrate the potency of PSBG as a photothermal conversion material. It can not only cause the release of inhibitors but can also generate heat energy to encourage the coating's barrier recovery effect when exposed to NIR light.

The equilibrium temperatures of several coating samples, when exposed to various power flux densities, are shown in Fig. 8B [11,48]. The equilibrium temperatures of the blank SMP coating own minimum value at all laser power densities due to the absence of a photothermal conversion medium. In contrast, all coatings based on nanofillers have surface temperatures that are higher than the Tg (about 45 °C) of the SMP matrix when the power density is only 1.0 W/cm2. It is evident that coatings based on nanofillers have good photothermal conversion capabilities, and the high temperature that is produced can help the SMP coating's healing properties. After 180 s of 2.0 W/cm2 irradiation, the surface plateau temperatures of Epoxy/1 wt% PS and Epoxy/1 wt% PSBG are 63 °C and 65 °C, respectively. On the other hand, the surface temperature of the SMP is dramatically raised with a modest increase in the content of nanofillers. 81 °C is reached as the surface temperature of the Epoxy/2 wt% PSBG coating after 180 s of radiation exposure. Surface temperatures of the coatings with Epoxy/1 wt% PS, Epoxy/1 wt% PSBG, and Epoxy/2 wt% PSBG coatings reach 95 °C, 97 °C, and 98 °C, respectively, when irradiation intensity is increased to 3.0 W/cm2. The PSBG nanofillers in the SMP demonstrate strong photothermal conversion capacity, which is a beneficial property for photothermally driven self-repairing coating, as shown by all of the aforementioned experiments [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Active corrosion protection study of the scratched coating samples
ce:section.ce:para:
Investigation of the active corrosion protection of the scratched Epoxy, Epoxy/1 wt% PS, Epoxy/1 wt% PSBG and Epoxy/2 wt% PSBG samples are done by the EIS test. The Z Simp Win software fits the EIS data to provide more electrochemical information. Repeated fitting of the data is performed until the chi-square is less than 10−3. Considering the actual electrochemical interface, the EIS data is fitted by two time constant electrical equivalent circuits (EECs) models ((R(Q(R(QR)))), shown in Fig. 12, and the acquired parameters (R
s,
CPE
c
, CPE
dl
, R
c
, R
ct
, n
c
, n
dl ) results are exhibited in Table S1–4. R
s stands for the solution resistance, R
c is the pore resistance of the coating, R
ct is the charge transfer resistance across the metal interface, CPE
c is the capacitance of the coating, and CPE
dl is the double-layer capacitance.

The Bode diagrams are displayed in Fig. 9. The |Z|
10mHz value of the blank SMP coating fluctuates in the range below 1.49 × 105 Ω cm2, at the same time Epoxy/1 wt% PS sample’s |Z|
10mHz value fluctuates in the range below 2.58 × 105 Ω cm2, during 72 h immersion tests. The result indicates those of the inhibitor-free SMP coating own poor active protection capability, which allows corrosive media to attack metal in a scratch continuously [18]. In contrast, the |Z|
10mHz values of the Epoxy/1 wt% PSBG and Epoxy/2 wt% PSBG are both experienced a significant increase during the immersion process, from 1.49 × 105 Ω cm2 to 3.03 × 105 Ω cm2, and from 1.29 × 105 Ω cm2 to 4.37 × 105 Ω cm2 respectively, which reflects the improvement in their active protection capability. The results demonstrating that BTA inhibitors can successfully be stimulated and released from PSBG. The variation of the |Z|
10mHz values throughout time can be compared in greater detail in Fig. 9E. Despite PSBG-based SMP coating, all lacking active substances coatings show a steady trend of |Z|
10mHz value. Conversely, the PSBG-based SMP coating’s value is higher than the inhibitor-free coatings, assigning to the self-released BTA can cover the forged scratch and provide protection for metal surfaces. The EIS findings provide strong support for the claim that PSBG-based SMP coatings have the best self-healing capabilities [50]. Generally, R
ct value can indicate the difficulty of charge transfer at the metal interface. A higher R
ct value frequently indicates a high-quality anti-corrosion layer, which is an extra sign of judgment used to assess the capacity for activity protection. The variation of fitted R
ct values is plotted in Fig. 9F. When coating lacking active inhibitor, the R
ct value shows a descending tendency during the 72 h of immersion such as pure Epoxy and Epoxy/1 wt% PS samples. While a growing time is being experienced by the R
ct value of composite coatings using PSBG fillers. The R
ct values are in the following order after 72 h: blank SMP coating < 1 wt% PS coating < 2 wt% PSBG coating ≈ 1 wt% PSBG coating. All of the aforementioned findings demonstrated that PSBG-based coating has an exceptional capacity for self-healing once more. This is an indisputable fact that the self-released BTA inhibitors positively bind up the AA2024 defect during the immersion period. Furthermore, the BTA film successfully inhibits the charge transfer. Additionally, evenly distributed nanofillers can stop corrosion ions from penetrating the coating through scratch locations. As a result, the two types of inhibitory action combine to protect the AA2024 substrate from corrosive environments. Furthermore, it is important to note that, in comparison to Epoxy/1 wt% PSBG, the R
ct value of the Epoxy/2 wt% PSBG sample exhibits a long-term upward trend because when compared to the coating with 1 wt% PSBG nanofillers, the coating with 2 wt% PSBG nanofillers can deliver more BTA molecules to delay corrosion activity.

Fig. S2A presents the Nyquist plots of various scratch coating samples immersed for 72 h. As demonstrated by the Epoxy/2 wt% PSBG and Epoxy/1 wt% in comparison to inhibitor-free coatings, PSBG-based coating demonstrates a larger capacitive arc, demonstrating its self-healing anti-corrosion capability. Furthermore, the diameter of capacitive arc of Epoxy/2 wt% PSBG is higher than Epoxy/1 wt% PSBG sample, explaining that the anti-corrosion ability of coatings had been improved by more BTA self-healing agent.

Additionally, high-capacity carbon atoms and a high aluminum-to-oxygen atom ratio of PSBG-based coatings successfully confirmed the release of BTA in the scratched area in the EDS results (Fig. S3) [51]. Meanwhile, The peak of chloride ion is much lower in Epoxy/1 wt% PSBG (Fig. S3C) and Epoxy/2 wt% PSBG (Fig. S3D) compared to the two other inhibitor-free samples, indicating that the inclusion of a corrosion inhibitor improves the coating's corrosion protection [52]. This can support our earlier EIS findings that the BTA molecule's stimulated discharge may be the root of the PSBG nanofiller samples' superior active corrosion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Barrier protection corrosion study of irradiated and healed coating samples
ce:section.ce:para:
To further determine the closure effect and anticorrosion performance of SMP composite coatings containing Epoxy, Epoxy/1 wt% PS, Epoxy/1 wt% PSBG and Epoxy/2 wt% PSBG coatings are manual damaged and then repaired under 10 min’ irradiation treatment for subsequent immersion experiments. EIS technique estimates the anticorrosion performance of healed coatings in a similar manner (shown in Fig. 10A-D). Compared with nano-PSBG healing agent samples, the presence of Epoxy’s the lowest |Z|
10mHz and lowest phase angle in the high-frequency region in 21d indicates that the scratch is still open [53], and the electrolyte can directly contact the metal substrate, causing the corrosive reaction to continue. It is surprising to observe that the nanofillers-based coating exhibits a complete recovery of the barrier characteristics following the irradiation healing process, as supported by both Bode and phase angle graphs. All of the nanofillers-based coatings’ |Z|
10mHz values restore to larger than 106 Ω cm2, which can reflect better excellent barrier anti-corrosion protection behaviors. Also, a short platform appears in the high-frequency area of the Bode diagram [54], because that PS and PSBG photothermal conversion materials may produce a lot of heat, which drives both the shape memory effect and the melting of the carnauba wax to cover the coating defects. The impedance modulus at 10 mHz for the Epoxy/1 wt% PS composite coating is a little lower than for the Epoxy/1 wt% PSBG and Epoxy/2 wt% PSBG coatings. It is obvious that the amino and carboxyl groups on the GQDs of nanofillers could improve the crosslinking density and integrity of the repaired coating [55]. On the other hand, the heat-induced GQD shed from the nanofillers' surface caused a significant amount of BTA to be released onto the metal surface, where it formed a layer. Based on the above reasons, Epoxy/1 wt% PSBG and Epoxy/2 wt% PSBG embodies a better corrosion protection effect. Additionally, the first seven days' |Z|
10mHz values of Epoxy/1 wt% PS is almost unaltered due to the absence of active protection agents. In sharp contrast, the coating containing 1 wt% and 2 wt% PSBG shows a slight rise. Meanwhile Epoxy/1 wt% PSBG and Epoxy/2 wt% PSBG exhibit overall higher |Z|
10mHz values and lower corruption tendency in the whole immersion experiment. Their impedance moduli at |Z|
10mHz are always above 4.2 × 106 Ω cm2, even after immersion for 21 days, demonstrating that the newly installed active anti-corrosion protective layer and the fully restored passive layer are working together to provide anti-corrosion protection [54]. In Fig. 10F, the R
ct value will wind down because the corrosive media can continuously damage the metal surface, but greater R
ct values can be seen in composite coatings based on PSBG. The order of R
ct values is blank epoxy coating < Epoxy/1 wt% PS coating < Epoxy/1 wt% PSBG coating < Epoxy/2 wt% PSBG coating. The aforementioned findings demonstrated once more how released BTA contributes to effective corrosion prevention. Generally speaking, the corrosion protection effect is maximized by inhibitor suppression and barrier reconstruction due to the synergistic effect of released BTA and the coating's high repair efficiency effect.

Fig. S2B shows the Nyquist plots of various healed coated samples that are submerged for 21 days. The greater capacitive arc of the nanocontainer-based coating at prolonged immersion testing can be attributed to the barrier recovery effect when compared to scratch coating and nanocontainer-free samples. Also, the capacitive arc of Epoxy/1 wt% PS coating is lower than Epoxy/1 wt% PSBG coating and Epoxy/2 wt% PSBG coating. This phenomenon can be attributed to the self-release of BTA inhibitors from PSBG, as shown in detail in Fig. 7, can further alleviate delamination of the coating and can limit corrosive species attack the metal surface.

The morphological recovery of the various types of SMP coatings is depicted in
Fig. 11 based on SEM observation. Apparently, each coating is individually applied with a 40 µm wide scratch before being exposed to NIR for 600 s. The low surface temperature, as shown in Fig. 8B, is the reason why the shape memory effect of blank SMP coating is generally limited and the breadth of the scratch region is still higher than 30 µm after irradiation. In contrast, all nanofillers-based coatings show a good closure effect. The fact that PS and PSBG nanofillers can both efficiently convert a significant amount of light into heat energy causes all scratches to be less than 2 µm. It is important to note that the Epoxy/2 wt% PSBG coating surface has some charring from being exposed to high temperatures for an extended period of time. In addition, the scrape entirely vanishes. This is in line with the electrochemical findings, which demonstrate the PSBG nanofillers' strong photothermal conversion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Coating morphology and mechanical property of the various coatings
ce:section.ce:para: One of the crucial factors for determining the mechanical durability of the coating is the adhesion performance of the coating/metal contact. In order to more accurately assess the coating adhesion performance, a cross-cut tape test is used. According to Fig. S4. The findings show that even after being scratched and peeled three times with tape, all coating samples maintained strong adherence to metal surfaces. The adhesion of the coating is rated as level 0 (excellent adhesive strength) because it is completely coated and does not exhibit any peeling phenomena [56]. Also, the cross-section SEM images of various coatings are shown in Fig. S5. There are no obvious cracks and defects in the coatings cross section. All the coating samples are tightly adhered to the metal surface. Consequently, the results confirmed that the addition of nanocontainers and carnauba wax microparticles will not reduce the mechanical property of epoxy coating for long-lasting anti-corrosion application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comprehensive self-repairing mechanism of PSBG-based coating
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, the schematic description as shown in Fig. 13, all coatings samples are both scratched, and then soak them in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for three days to verify their self-repairing and active protection ability. After that, they are irradiated for 10 min to verify their light and heat healing ability.

When the coating is damaged, the electrolyte will penetrate into the scratch area of the coating, which normally initiates the alterations of the microregion pH value on the basis of the below redox corrosion reactions [38]:
(1) Anode:Al +3Cl-+3 H2O→Al (OH)3+3HCl
(2) Cathode: 2 H2O+O2+4e-→4OH-

The changed pH value can increase electrostatic repulsion between the GQDs cap and PS nanocontainers, causing the massive dissociation of GQDs from the PS nanofillers. After that, the electrolyte and free BTA flow to the corrosion site where they chemisorb and physisorb to form a coating on the corrosion zone. This film serves as a dense barrier that can isolate corrosive medium and decelerate further corrosion reaction. For irradiated coating samples, the high passive and active anti-corrosion protection ability can be summarized into the following three points. Firstly, PSBG composite nanofillers can dramatically raise the coating's temperature thanks to their powerful photothermal conversion, which melts carnauba wax microparticles to fix coating defects and activates the SMP coating's closure effect. Secondly, at high temperatures, the hydrogen bond between the GQDs cap and PS container can be cut off, causing the active self-release of BTA to further protect the alloys. Finally, the healed PSBG-based coatings own a higher anti-corrosion performance since the presence of the functional groups on the GQDs surface can improve nanofillers’ dispersion and compatibility in SMP coating. Consequently, based on the above reasons, the irradiated coating has been completely repaired which can exert its excellent barrier protection effect to limit aggressive species contact with the metal substrate. Consequently, the PSBG structures in SMP coating could automatically sense the change in pH near the cathode and anode and further provide a large number of BTA to slow down corrosion reaction [57]. Additionally, the defects may be precisely rectified with the aid of irradiation, which is helpful for the transcendence of long-term anti-corrosion performance.

In this contribution, we ingeniously synthesized a novel pH and photothermal response PSBG composite nanofillers, which endow the shape memory epoxy with pH and photothermal response dual-action self-healing abilities. Long-term corrosion protection for AA2024 is possible with such an intelligent coating. The SEM/FTIR/XPS/Zeta potential/UV–vis results confirmed that the construction of as-prepared GQDs capped PS nanocontainers (PSBG) is a multi-functional nanofillers, which can control inhibitors release and possess strong light-heat conversion. EIS tests results show that the log |Z|
0.01 Hz value of the defect PSBG coating is nearly increased by 0.3 orders of magnitude, illustrating its good active corrosion protection. Meanwhile, healed PSBG coating is about 2 orders of magnitude higher than pure SMP, proving its excellent barrier corrosion protection. Corresponding SEM results also clarified scratches are covered with corrosion inhibitor and scratch size is successfully healed by incorporated photothermal PSBG material. In addition, in the actual application scenario, when the coating is exposed to sunlight, the PSBG-based coating's photothermal conversion could significantly speed up the evaporation of corrosive liquid, drastically lowering the amount of corrosion medium that enters the coated substrate. Therefore, the PSBG nanofillers healing agent would be a meaningful strategy for enhancing the self-healing efficiency and anti-corrosion performance of SMP coating.

Xianliang Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Data curation, Formal analysis. Bin Gong: Methodology, Validation. Jie Zhang: Methodology. Yuyang Di: Validation. Yan Ding: Methodology. Zhihao Chen: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Wenzhong Yang: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Project administration.
